coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the interaction between carbon nanodot and IR light emitting fluorescent dyes inside the confined micellar environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Dutta, Rupam
Bhattacharya, Sayantan
Pyne, Arghajit
Datta, Prasanta Kumar
Sarkar, Nilmoni
coredata.dc:description: In this contribution, using microwave heating of polyethylene glycol (PEG 200), we have prepared carbon nanodot (CND). It is found that in aqueous medium as well as in the micellar solutions of triblock copolymer, P123 and in anionic surface active ionic liquid (SAIL), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium n-octylsulfate ([C4mim][C8SO4]) the fluorescence intensity of CND gets quenched in the presence of cationic cyanine dye HITCP and cationic phenoxazine dye Nile blue (NB). Stern-Volmer constant indicates that the quenching of fluorescence intensity is more in the aqueous medium compared to P123 and [C4mim][C8SO4] i.e., (BmimOs) micelles. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurement reveals that the fluorescence quenching is static in nature and the quenching through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process is not dominant here. Addition of both the cationic dye molecules (HITCP and NB) in CND solution generates new excited state absorption peaks in transient absorption measurement. This observation suggests that a transient species is generated through photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between the donor-acceptor pairs and this PET is responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CNDs by the dye molecules. Moreover, this electron transfer kinetics is relatively faster in bulk water compared to that inside P123 and BmimOs micelle due to the intervention of the surfactant molecules between CND and the dye molecules. Therefore, this study provides a new insight about the PET dynamics between CND and fluorescent dye molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
Triblock copolymer
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon nanodot (CND) loaded P123 micelles
Preparation of carbon nanodot (CND) loaded cationic and anionic SAIL micelles
Characterization of carbon nanodot (CND) loaded P123, cationic and anionic micelles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Steady-state (a) absorption and (b) emission spectra (λ ex = 340 nm) of PEG and dialysed CNDs (c) FTIR spectra (d) Raman spectra (e) DLS size distribution histogram of dialysed CNDs (f) and (g) HR-TEM images of dialysed CNDs (h) size distribution histogram of dialysed CNDs obtained from HR-TEM images (Fig. 1f) using ImageJ software.
(a) DLS size distribution histograms of P123 micelle and CND incorporated P123 micelle, HR-TEM images of (b) P123 micelle and (c) CND incorporated P123 micelle.
Excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra of CND in (a) water (b) P123 micelle.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CND (λ ex = 340 nm) in the presence of HITCP in (a) water (b) P123 micelle, and fluorescence emission spectra of CND (λ ex = 340 nm) in the presence of Nile blue in (c) water (d) P123 micelle (Stern-Volmer plots are shown in inset).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CND (λ ex = 375 nm) in the presence of HITCP in (a) water (b) P123 micelle, (c) and BmimOs micelle and fluorescence emission spectra of CND (λ ex = 375 nm) in the presence of Nile blue in (d) water (e) P123 micelle, (f) and BmimOs micelle (Stern-Volmer plots are shown in inset).
Fluorescence decay profiles of CND (λ ex = 375 nm) in (a) water (b) P123 micelle and (c) BmimOs micelle in the presence of Nile blue and HITCP.

Transient absorption spectra of CND in the presence of HITCP (λ ex = 632 nm) in (a) water (b) P123 micelle, and transient absorption spectra of CND in the presence of Nile blue (λ ex = 632 nm) in (c) water (d) P123 micelle.
The chemical structures of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium n-octylsulfate ([C4mim][C8SO4]), 1-dodecyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (C12mimCl), HITC Perchlorate (HITCP) and Nile blue A Perchlorate (NB).
Electron transfer pathways of CND in the presence of fluorescent dye molecules (HITCP and NB).
Stern–Volmer quenching constants due to the interaction of CND with two different quenchers (HITCP and Nile blue) in bulk water and inside various micelles at excitation wavelengths 340 and 375 nm.
Time-resolved fluorescence parameters of CND in water, inside P123 and BmimOs micelle in the presence of two different quenchers (HITCP and Nile blue) (λ ex = 375 nm).

head.ce:title: Unveiling the interaction between carbon nanodot and IR light emitting fluorescent dyes inside the confined micellar environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, WB, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, WB, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this contribution, using microwave heating of polyethylene glycol (PEG 200), we have prepared carbon nanodot (CND). It is found that in aqueous medium as well as in the micellar solutions of triblock copolymer, P123 and in anionic surface active ionic liquid (SAIL), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium n-octylsulfate ([C4mim][C8SO4]) the fluorescence intensity of CND gets quenched in the presence of cationic cyanine dye HITCP and cationic phenoxazine dye Nile blue (NB). Stern-Volmer constant indicates that the quenching of fluorescence intensity is more in the aqueous medium compared to P123 and [C4mim][C8SO4] i.e., (BmimOs) micelles. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurement reveals that the fluorescence quenching is static in nature and the quenching through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process is not dominant here. Addition of both the cationic dye molecules (HITCP and NB) in CND solution generates new excited state absorption peaks in transient absorption measurement. This observation suggests that a transient species is generated through photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between the donor-acceptor pairs and this PET is responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CNDs by the dye molecules. Moreover, this electron transfer kinetics is relatively faster in bulk water compared to that inside P123 and BmimOs micelle due to the intervention of the surfactant molecules between CND and the dye molecules. Therefore, this study provides a new insight about the PET dynamics between CND and fluorescent dye molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials such as metal nanoclusters, quantum dots, etc. have received a lot of attention in the field of nanotechnology and medicine in the recent decades [1,2]. Of late, carbon nanodot (CND) appears as a rapidly advancing and expanding research field and this new class of promising fluorescent probe, CND can potentially substitute toxic traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) owing to their high aqueous solubility, benign nature, chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility and unique optical properties such as tunable photoluminescence, high resistance to photobleaching, less optical blinking, excitation dependent multicolor emission and so forth [3–5]. These properties arise due to the quantum confinement effect [3,6] as well as due to the presence of different chromophoric groups in CND [7]. Moreover, CNDs have application in optical bio-imaging, bio-sensing, photo-catalysis, photovoltaics, and optoelectronics [8]. Besides, it can act as nanocarrier in cancer cells [2,9] on account of several surface functionalities like amines, amides, alcoholic hydroxyls, esters, carbonyl, etc [10]. The mechanism of photoluminescence property of CND may be due to the energy trapping on the passivated surface of carbon particle [11]. However, the photoluminescence from CND can be quenched by various mechanisms [12]. Quenching mechanism includes static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and so forth. Here, it is relevant to mention that CND can act both as an excellent electron donor as well as electron acceptor and therefore it has an extensive application in light energy conversion and related areas [13–15]. Thus, the electron transfer mechanism plays a major role in the fluorescence quenching of CNDs in the presence of suitable quencher. PET has an important contribution to numerous chemical and biological processes [14,16]. Purkayastha and co-workers have extensively studied electron donating and acceptance property of CNDs with well-known electron acceptor methyl viologen (MV2+) and with electron donor N, N dimethylaniline (DMA), respectively in different confined media like reverse micelle and in other environments [13,17,18].

Nile blue (NB) is a nontoxic, cationic, fluorescent phenoxazine dye with heterocyclic planar and rigid structure. The hydrophobic, planar phenoxazine moiety can potentially intercalate in the nonpolar major groove of DNA helix [19]. NB has the advantage of low cytotoxicity and high sensitivity compared to the conventional intercalators [20]. Moreover, intermolecular electron transfer between acceptor NB and donor solvents aniline, N, N dimethylaniline (DMA) and N, N diethylaniline (DEA) are well reported [21,22]. NB can also photosensitize DNA damage by accepting the electron from donor guanine moiety of DNA [19]. Baigar et al. and Feilitzsch et al. have shown the intramolecular electron transfer from ferrocene donor moiety to NB acceptor [23,24]. The Yang group has shown intermolecular electron transfer from glutathione capped CdTe quantum dots to NB [25]. On the other hand, cationic polymethine dyes have potential application as a photosensitizer for cancer photochemotherapy [26]. The Kochevar group and other groups have reported that excited polymethine dye molecules generate highly reactive radicals and most probably through this mechanism they damage the cancer cells [27]. Indotricarbocyanine dyes are mainly localized in regions of low dielectric permeability in cancer cells and present primarily as tight ion pairs [28]. 1,1′,3,3,3′,3′-hexamethyl-2,2′-indotricarbocyanine perchlorate (HITCP) is also a near infra-red (NIR) light emitting symmetric, cationic cyanine dye and exhibits the above-mentioned properties. This NIR wavelength regime minimises the absorbance of heme in hemoglobin and myoglobin, water, and lipids and therefore these cyanine dyes are more important for fluorescence imaging of deep tissues [29]. Nowadays the self-assembly of non-ionic amphiphilic triblock copolymers (TBPs) are extensively used in different chemical and biological purposes [30]. Hydrophilic Poly-(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and hydrophobic poly-(propylene oxide) (PPO) can form different PEO-PPO-PEO TBPs depending on the composition of PEO and PPO groups. The TBP of the type (PEO)20–(PPO)70–(PEO)20 is known as P123. Hydrophobic PPO units remain in the core region whereas hydrophilic PEO units are present in the micellar surface. Non-toxic, chemically stable pluronic TBPs are widely used to prepare different self-assembled aggregates like micelles, mixed micelles, etc [31]. Nowadays, surface active ionic liquids (SAILs) are extensively used to obtain various self-assemblies as they have the surface active property along with the unique properties of ionic liquids [32–34]. Interestingly, imidazolium moiety containing ionic liquids are superior to conventional surfactants having alkyl group [33].

Herein, we have prepared CNDs by the microwave heating of polyethylene glycol (PEG 200). Fuorescence quenching of CNDs are found in the presence of cationic cyanine dye HITCP and cationic phenoxazine dye Nile blue in bulk water as well as in P123 and in anionic SAIL, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium n-octylsulfate ([C4mim][C8SO4]) containing micellar medium. From the TCSPC and transient absorption measurements, we have concluded that the quenching is static in nature and passes through electron transfer pathway. Moreover, the quenching is faster in bulk water compared to P123 micelle and anionic [C4mim][C8SO4] micelle as the surfactant molecules retard the electron transfer kinetics.

PEG 200 and the triblock copolymer P123 were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and used as received to prepare carbon nanodot (CND) and micellar solution, respectively. Surface active ionic liquids (SAILs), 1-dodecyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (C12mimCl) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium n-octylsulfate ([C4mim][C8SO4]) or BmimOs, were purchased from Kanto Chemicals (98% purity) and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively. HITCP and Nile blue A perchlorate were received from Exciton. Milli-Q water was used to prepare all the solutions and all the experiments were performed at 298 K. The chemical structures of the dyes are shown in Scheme 1 .

Steady-state absorption and emission study, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) and transient absorption measurements were performed. Steady-state absorption and fluorescence measurements were performed using a Shimadzu (model UV 2450) UV–Vis spectrophotometer and a Spex Fluorolog-3 (model FL3-11) spectrofluorimeter, respectively.

We have carried out ultrafast transient absorption measurements using commercially available transient absorption spectrometer from Newport Corp. We have used a Ti: Sapphire (Coherent Inc, Libra) regenerative amplifier laser system for this experiment as the main laser source. The output of used regenerative amplifier has been centered at 808 nm, with average pulse energy ˜3 mJ at the repetition rate of 1 kHz and pulse width is around 50 fs. A beam splitter was used to split the whole beam into two parts 70/30 (R/T). The pump beam at 350 and 632 nm were generated from an optical parametric amplifier (TOPAS-PRIME, Light Conversion) using a major portion of the beam and was used to generate a white-light continuum (WLC) probe beam having spectral range of 350–850 nm by focusing it onto a 3-mm CaF2 crystal a small portion of the fundamental beam was allowed to pass through a computer-controlled motorized delay stage (Newport Corp, ILS-LM). On the sample pump and probe beams were focused with the spot size of 210 μm and 80 μm, respectively. We can get maximum time delay between pump and probe around 3 ns. WLC probe was collected by a fiber-coupled spectrometer after passing through the sample, with a Si photodiode array (Oriel Instrument, MS-260i). Samples were placed in the cuvette having path length 1 mm for the measurement and with a magnetic stirring system they were continuously stirred. Block diagram of the instrument is provided in Scheme S1 (Supporting Information). The detailed description of the DLS, HR-TEM and TCSPC instruments are given in the Supporting Information.

Carbon nanodot (CND) was prepared by mixing polyethylene glycol (PEG200) and milli-Q water in 3:1 (v/v) ratio in a conical flask. Thereafter the mixed solution was heated in an 800 W domestic microwave oven (Panasonic) for 5 min. Generation of a yellow coloured solution was the indication of the formation of CNDs. Microwave irradiation technique was chosen as it is rapid, eco-friendly and cost-effective. Several purification techniques of CNDs like silica column chromatography [35], dialysis [36], etc. are well reported. To remove the reactant molecules (here PEG 200) and molecular by products efficiently from the fluorescence emitter CNDs, we have used dialysis as the efficient separation technique with the aid of membrane having molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of 1 kDa for 5 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon nanodot (CND) loaded P123 micelles
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the appropriate amount of P123 copolymer was weighed and it was dissolved in milli-Q water in a volumetric flask to prepare 5 wt % stock solution of the P123 micelle and the mixture was stirred overnight with a magnetic stirrer and the solution was kept for one day before use. Thereafter the dialysed CND was mixed with the aqueous solution of P123 in 1:1 volumetric ratio and the mixture was kept for one day for the proper incorporation of CNDs inside the micelle and after that the entire spectroscopic and microscopic studies were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon nanodot (CND) loaded cationic and anionic SAIL micelles
ce:section.ce:para: The cmc’s (critical micelle concentrations) of C12mimCl and [C4mim][C8SO4] i.e., BmimOs are ∼14.22 mM and ∼31 mM, respectively [32]. Therefore, the stock solutions of 40 mM concentration of C12mimCl and BmimOs were prepared separately in water. Thereafter the prepared CND was mixed with the aqueous solution of the micellar aggregates of SAILs in 1:1 volumetric ratio and the mixture was kept for one day for the proper incorporation of CNDs inside the micelle and after that the entire spectroscopic studies were performed.

The CND sample after dialysis is characterized from the absorption spectra shown in Fig. 1 a. CNDs typically show optical absorption in the UV region with a tail extending to the visible range. The peak ∼215 nm is the π-π* transition peak for aromatic CC bonds and a weak shoulder ∼300 nm indicates the n-π* transition for CO bonds [37]. CNDs exhibit blue fluorescence with emission maxima ∼465 nm upon excitation at 340 nm, shown in Fig. 1b. We have performed Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Raman measurements to shed light on the structure of the CNDs. Fig. 1c shows the presence of different functional groups on the surface of CNDs. The sharp peak at 1102 cm−1 is due to the epoxy (-C-O-C-) stretching frequency whereas the peak at 1634 cm−1 can be attributed to the presence of > CC < bond. Other peaks at 1719, 3040 and 3412 cm−1 represent carbonyl (>CO), alkenyl(sp2) CH and OH stretching frequencies, respectively. The presence of hydrophilic hydroxyl (OH) group at the surface of CNDs enhances its polarity and thus the CNDs are readily water soluble [35]. In the Raman spectra, we have found two bands D and G at around 1352 and 1596 cm-1, respectively and shown in Fig. 1d. D band represents the graphitic carbon and appears due to the breathing modes of the sp2 atoms whereas G band denotes oxidized carbon and arises as a result of the bond stretching of all pairs of sp2 atoms [38,39]. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique shows a single size distribution histogram and the average size is ∼5.8 ± 2 nm, shown in Fig. 1e. We have further confirmed the formation of CND by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) technique and depicted in Fig. 1f, g. Thereafter using ImageJ software, we have obtained a size distribution histogram (for Fig. 1f) and the average size of the particle is ∼4.6 ± 1.5 nm, shown in Fig. 1h. In DLS measurement, we obtain the hydrodynamic diameter of the particle in solution phase. On the other hand, in HR-TEM measurement we get the size of the particle in dried condition and may be due to this reason there is some difference in size of the particles in two techniques.

Various groups have recently revealed the importance and urgency of a unified nomenclature to classify the different fluorescent nanocarbons such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) depending upon their physical, chemical and the photophysical properties [40–42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon nanodot (CND) loaded P123, cationic and anionic micelles
ce:section.ce:para: To get the idea about the incorporation of CNDs inside the relatively hydrophobic P123 micelles [30], we have performed DLS measurement, shown in Fig. 2 a. DLS size distribution histogram indicates that the average size of the P123 micelle is ∼12-15 nm with a sharp single size-distribution. With the increase in the amount of CNDs, the size of the CND loaded P123 aggregates increase up to ∼40-60 nm and this clearly dictates that CNDs are efficiently incorporating inside the P123 micelle and the shape of the aggregates remain as spherical. This P123 micelle stabilizes the hydrophilic CNDs via hydrophilic-hydrophilic interactions between the PEO units of the hydrophilic corona region and the hydrophilic surface of the CNDs [30,43]. This hydrophilic interaction is the driving force for the CNDs to go inside the micelle. To gain further insight into the shape and size of the aggregates, we have performed HR-TEM measurement and the images of the P123 micelle and CND encapsulated P123 micelles are depicted in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. Though, HR-TEM images provide the shape of the aggregate, DLS size distribution histograms provide better picture about the size increment of the micelle.

The aggregation phenomena of C12mimCl and BmimOs in water are very different from each other. For C12mimCl, the dodecyl chain containing cationic part behaves very similar to that of the conventional cationic surfactants. On the other hand, it is reported that, in aqueous medium BmimOs shows dual transition in physical properties [44]. Anionic aggregation is the reason of first transition and the alkyl chain containing imidazolium cation penetrates toward the micellar core resulting in the second transition. The intensity-size distribution histograms of C12mimCl and BmimOs containing micellar aggregates in the presence of CND are shown in Fig. S1a and S1b, respectively. DLS size distribution histograms indicate that the average size of C12mimCl and BmimOs containing micelles are ∼2.7-3 nm with a sharp single size-distribution. With the increase in the amount of CNDs, the size of the CND loaded micelles increase up to ∼4-4.2 nm and this confirms the incorporation of CNDs inside the micelle. The size of the micellar aggregates consist of SAILs, depends on the relative occupancy and nature of the counterions [32]. For the anionic micelle BmimOs or [C4mim][C8SO4], the counterion [C4mim]+ is larger than that of the Cl− anion of C12mimCl. Moreover, the [C4mim]+ cation mostly remains embedded in the micellar core region of [C4mim][C8SO4] whereas the location of the counterion Cl− anion of C12mimCl is the micellar interface [44]. Therefore, in spite of having the different number of carbon atoms on the chains of their cationic part, the total number of carbons contributing toward the hydrophobic domain of the both the micelles is same. The average diameter of C12mimCl and BmimOs micelles are around ∼2.7 nm; however, a minor peak population in the larger diameter range is also found due to the presence of few larger aggregates.

The origin of the optical properties of CNDs can be described on the basis of two prevalent mechanisms-polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) domain and smaller sized molecular fluorophore. It is well-reported that synthesized fluorescent CND samples obtained from bottom-up as well as microwave synthesis contain two distinct types of fluorophores, one having the smaller size (<1 kDa) with high quantum yield i.e., molecular fluorophore and the other one with larger size (≥50 kDa) having low quantum yield i.e., carbogenic core of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH). Very similar to the CNDs, molecular fluorophores also possess sp2/sp3 carbon domains and aggregates through the stacking interaction [45].

In the Fig. 3 a,b, we have shown the excitation wavelength dependent emission of CNDs in bulk water and inside P123 micellar aggregates varying the excitation wavelength from 325 nm to 525 nm. We have observed that with the increase in the excitation wavelength, the emission peak maxima are also red-shifted with diminish in the emission intensity after a maximum value. The sizes of the formed CNDs are not uniform and the presence of different functional groups (CO, CO, OCOH) at the surface of CNDs make different surface states and these are the probable reasons behind the alteration of the emission maxima with the change in excitation wavelength. Moreover ∼3 nm blue shift in the fluorescence spectra of CNDs inside the P123micelle compared to aqueous solution further confirms incorporation of CNDs inside the P123 micelle. (Fig. 3)

After gathering sufficient evidences about the formation of CNDs in bulk water as well as in P123 micellar media, we are interested to study the interaction of CNDs with a cationic cyanine dye HITCP and a cationic phenoxazine dye Nile blue. CND can be excited at different wavelengths and we have found that excitation at 340 and 375 nm provides the similar result in steady state emission measurements in the presence of the two cationic dye molecules. At these two excitations none of the two dye molecules are excited as the dyes have no absorption at this wavelength. Therefore, excitation at 340 and 375 nm specifically excites the CND and with the gradual addition of HITCP and NB, the fluorescence intensity of CND decreases in both aqueous and P123 micellar media. A systematic investigation of photoluminescence quenching of CND (λ ex = 340 nm) by increasing the concentration of cationic cyanine dye HITCP in bulk water and in P123 micelles are shown in Fig. 4 a,b. Similarly, the fluorescence quenching of CNDs (λ ex = 340 nm) by progressive addition of NB is also observed by exciting CNDs in aqueous and micellar media and depicted in Fig. 4c,d. To get an idea about the surface charge of the SAIL containing micelles on the fluorescence quenching of the CND by the dye molecules, we have performed the similar study using cationic SAIL C12mimCl and anionic SAIL BmimOs. Very interestingly, it is found that both the cationic dyes (HITCP and Nile blue) are unable to quench the fluorescence intensity of CND when CND is incorporated inside the cationic micelle of C12mimCl, shown in Fig. S2a,b. On the other hand, increase in concentration of the cationic dye molecules after the incorporation of CND in anionic BmimOs quenches the fluorescence intensity; however the extent of quenching efficiency is relatively low compared to P123 micelle, shown in Fig. S2c, d. Therefore, it is quite evident that the surface charges of micelles have a dominant role on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CND by the cationic dye molecules. Major numbers of the cationic dye molecules (HITCP and Nile blue) are located at the polar surface region of the micellar aggregates. In the cationic micelle, there is the possibility of electrostatic repulsion between the cationic dye molecules with the surface of the micelle. As a result, the dye molecules (HITCP and Nile blue) cannot efficiently penetrate inside the micelle and thus almost no quenching is found for C12mimCl. On the other hand, for the anionic micelle BmimOs, the cationic dye molecules are strongly attracted towards the polar micellar surface through favourable electrostatic interaction and can quench the fluorescence of CND. However, the quenching efficiency is relatively less in BmimOs micelle compared to P123 micelle and this is possibly due to the fact that most of the cationic dye molecules are strongly bound in the micellar surface, resulting in lesser extent of quenching [46–48]. Similarly, the fluorescence quenching study of CND in water, P123 and BmimOs micelles in the presence of HITCP and Nile blue dyes, exciting CND at 375 nm are shown in Fig. 5 a-f. The Fayer group has shown the PET dynamics between a suitable donor-acceptor pair in different cationic micelles of varying chain length and concluded that electron transfer rate gets enhanced with the increase in the micellar radius [47]. In all the studies, we have added the dyes up to 15 μM concentration to avoid any self-aggregation of the individual dyes to form the dimer or higher order aggregates at higher concentration. We have discussed earlier that the fluorescence of CNDs can be quenched by energy transfer as well as by the electron transfer pathway. The basic criteria for the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is the substantial amount of overlap between the fluorescence spectra of donor moiety and the absorption spectra of the acceptor one. In our study, FRET process may also take place as there is some spectral overlapping region for the emission spectra of CND (donor for FRET) and absorption spectra of HITCP or Nile blue (acceptor for FRET), shown in Fig. S3a,d. However, our lifetime measurement reveals that there is no decrease in the lifetime of CND in the presence of the dyes (HITCP or Nile blue) as expected for the donor molecules of FRET process. Therefore, we can rule out the possibility of fluorescence quenching of CNDs through energy transfer route.

The quenching follows the Stern-Volmer relation
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
Q

The fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher, Q (HITCP and Nile blue) are represented by F0 and F, respectively. K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. The extent of fluorescence quenching is measured from the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (K SV), which is obtained from the slope of the plot of F0/F vs [Q]. The Stern-Volmer plots are shown in the inset and the K SV values for quenching (λ ex = 340 nm) by HITCP dye are 8.1, 5.26 and 4.05 mM−1 in bulk water, P123 micelle, and BmimOs micelle, respectively. On the other hand, K SV values are 16.61, 9.81, and 7.45 mM−1 for NB quencher in bulk water, P123 micelle, and BmimOs micelle, respectively. From the K SV values, (both for λ ex = 340 and λ ex = 375 nm) shown in Table 1 , it is evident that K SV value is higher for both the quenchers (HITCP and NB) in bulk water compared to P123 and BmimOs micelle. Moreover, inside P123 micelle quenching constant is higher than BmimOs micelle as no electrostatic attraction is present between the micellar surface of P123 and the dye molecules. It is also found that, NB has the higher quenching efficiency compared to the HITCP dye. Pal and co-workers have reported that inside the organized assemblies, electron transfer process gets highly retarded compared to bulk solvent [49]. In the micellar aggregates, intervention of the surfactant chains between the electron donor and acceptor pair causes a larger separation between the pair as well as lowers the electronic coupling and thus the electron transfer takes place in a highly non-diffusive manner [50].

The lower K SV value in P123 micelle compared to bulk water also indicates the electron transfer mechanism to dominate the quenching process. The Vodnik group has reported the fluorescence quenching of an anionic cyanine dye by gold nanoparticle and this quenching can be ascribed as energy transfer or electron transfer [51]. Our group has also reported that the reason behind the fluorescence quenching of gold nanoclusters by the different cyanine dyes is the involvement of electron transfer mechanism where the cyanine dyes act as electron donor [52]. Purkayastha and co-workers have studied the PET dynamics between CNDs and cyclometalated Rhodium and Iridium complexes [53]. Moreover, they have shown pH-triggered reversible PET pathway where CND can act both as an electron donor as well as an acceptor [15].

Along with steady-state measurements, we have performed the time-resolved measurements to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CND by the quencher dye molecules. Lifetime measurement provides the information about the nature of quenching i.e., static or dynamic. For static quenching, no change in the fluorescence lifetime of the CND takes place as the complex forms in the ground state. Fan et al. have shown the static quenching through ground state complex formation and the involvement of PET mechanism in this process [54]. Purakayastha and co-workers have also reported the ground state complex formation between the CND and other molecules and the participation of PET mechanism between CNDs with suitable electron acceptors [17,53].

In static quenching, electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions play the dominant role to form the ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quencher. In this study, the prepared CND has almost neutral charge and the quencher dye molecules (HITCP, Nile blue) are positively charged; therefore hydrophobic interaction might lead to the formation of ground-state complex. However, in the Fig. S4a,b we have found no trace of ground-state complex formation. Therefore, PET may be the dominant mechanism for fluorescence quenching rather than the ground-state complex formation. Here, static quenching mechanism is operating may be due to the close proximity of the donor-acceptor pair. In dynamic quenching, the lifetime of the fluorophore decreases as energy transfer or electron transfer takes place during the collision [55,56]. We have used 340 and 375 nm diode laser to excite CNDs in the presence and absence of the two quencher molecules (HITCP and NB) in bulk water, P123 and BmimOs micellar aggregates and the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves are depicted in Fig. S5a-c and Fig. 6 a–c, respectively.

Fluorescence decay profiles of CND can be adequately fitted by a sum of three exponentials
(2)
<
τ
a v
> =
a
1
τ
1
+
a
2
τ
2
+
a
3
τ
3
where,
τ 1
,
τ 2
, a n d
τ
3
are the three lifetime components, respectively and
a 1
,
a
2
,
a
3
are the corresponding contributions. This type of multi-component fitting suggests that multiple radiative species are present in the system and therefore we have considered the average lifetime (
< τ
a v
> ).

It is observed from Table 2 , that the lifetime of carbon nanodot (CND) increases inside the P123 micelle compared to water in the presence and absence of the fluorescent dye molecules (HITCP and Nile blue). However, the corresponding contributions or weightings (a1, a2 and a3) remain almost unchanged and this suggests that the population of the multiple radiative species remain same in the presence and absence of P123 micelle. But, the increment in lifetime infers the enhancement of hydrohobicity in the surroundings of the probe molecules inside P123 micelle compared to the bulk water.

Fluorescence decay times of CND (λ ex = 340 nm) in bulk water in the presence of HITCP and Nile blue are shown in Table S1, which indicates that the average lifetime of CND in water (1.38 ns) remains almost constant in the presence and absence of the quencher molecules. Similarly, the average lifetime of CND in P123 and BmimOs micellar aggregates in the presence of the same two quencher molecules also remains same and it is shown in Table S1. As the lifetime of CND remain unaltered in the presence and absence of the quenchers in bulk water, P123 and BmimOs micellar aggregate, and no deviation from linearity in the Stern-Volmer plot in the concentration range used herein, we can conclude that quenching in fluorescence intensity is unambiguously due to static quenching mechanism. We have chosen 340 nm as the excitation wavelength and its full width at half maximum (fwhm) is ∼930 ps. Lifetime measurement of CND (λ ex = 340 nm) in water, P123 and BmimOs micelle in the presence of two fluorescent dyes (HITCP and Nile blue) dyes show almost no change in the lifetime. Thus, the excitation at 340 nm does not provide any proof about the incorporation of CNDs inside the micelle. Therefore, we have chosen 375 nm diode laser as the excitation source whose fwhm is ∼165 ps and the lifetime values are shown in Table 2. It is observed that the lifetime of CND increases from ∼1.45 to ∼2.1 ns (i.e., around 600 ps) in going from water to P123 and BmimOs micelle. This observation suggests that CNDs are efficiently incorporating inside the micelle. However, addition of the fluorescent dyes to water, and micellar medium does not show any change in the lifetime and further confirms the involvement of static quenching mechanism. The change in rate of the non-radiative deactivation of the excited state can be calculated by the following equation
(3)
k q
=
1 τ
−
1
τ 0
where
τ and
τ
0
are the decay times of CND in the presence and absence of the quencher. The change in the rate of the non-radiative relaxation are 0.73 × 107 s−1 and 1.05 × 107 s-1 for 15 μM HITCP and 15 μM NB addition, respectively in bulk water (λ ex = 340 nm). On the other hand, the k q values are 0.47 × 107 s−1 and 0.62 × 107 s−1 for the same concentrations of HITCP and NB addition, respectively in the P123 micelle. For the BmimOs micelle the k q values are 0.36 × 107 s−1 and 0.47 × 107 s-1 for the addition HITCP and NB, respectively. Therefore, quenching of CND fluorescence by the HITCP and NB dye molecules is static in nature and electron transfer mechanism is responsible for this quenching process. Literature reports also support this type of static quenching through electron transfer pathway for quantum dots and gold nanoparticles [55,57].

Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) dynamics initiates through the initial excitation of the donor molecule from the ground state to the excited state and subsequent electron transfer to the surrounding acceptor molecule through the coupling between the donor and acceptor states and form radical ions. For PET process, overlap integral depends on the distance and relative orientation of the molecular orbitals of the donor-acceptor pair [58]. It is also well studied that CND has both the properties of electron donation as well as electron acceptance. Therefore, to get the better information about the fate of quenching of fluorescence intensity of CNDs by the dye molecules through electron transfer pathway, we have performed transient absorption measurements. Here, we have recorded the transient absorption spectra at different delay times following 632 nm pulsed excitation. The absorption and emission spectra of 15 μM HITCP appear at 746 and 768 nm, respectively in bulk water and shown in Fig. S6a. It is reported that, upon photoexcitation cationic polymethine cyanine dyes can form free radicals through charge transfer process and these free radicals can damage the cancer cells [28]. Being, an indotricarbocyanine dye, HITCP also forms HITC•+ cation radical upon photoexcitation [28].

On addition of 15 μM HITCP solution, in the aqueous solution of CND, the spectral feature for excited state absorption (ESA) appear at 491 and 527 nm. On the other hand, when 15 μM HITCP solution is added in the P123 micellar solution of CND, the positive signal in the

ΔOD vs wavelength spectrum appears at around 492 and 529 nm. The growth after the addition of HITCP in the solution of CND in bulk water and inside P123 micellar environments are shown in Fig. 7 a and 7b, respectively. However, the growth appears at 490 and 526 nm when HITCP is added after the incorporation of CND in BmimOs micelle, shown in Fig. S7a.These peaks clearly provide decisive evidence that a new transient species has been generated after the irradiation of HITCP in the presence of CND. The appearance of this positive signal is due to formation of a stable HITC•+ cation radical through the electron donation to CND as reported by Samtsov and co-workers [28]. Here, stimulated emission (SE) and ground state bleaching (GSB) bands appear within 650–800 nm regions in both the medium and shown in Fig. S8a. When we have added HITCP solution to the aqueous solution of CND, a transient absorption peak appears at ∼491 nm within ∼0.5 ps of the excitation followed by a gradual decay in higher delay times, whereas the maximum growth in transient absorption peak appears at ∼492 nm in a relatively longer delay time (∼1 ps) for the P123 micellar aggregate and ∼1.6 ps for BmimOs micelle.

Therefore, for P123 and BmimOs micelle more time is needed to form the transient species through electron transfer as the electron transfer kinetics is strongly retarded inside the confined media of P123 and BmimOs micelle due to the intervention of the surfactant molecules between CND and HITCP [59].

On the other hand, Nile blue is a well-studied benzophenoxazine dye and shows absorption and emission maxima at 635 and 664 nm, respectively and shown in Fig. S6b. Hirakawa et al. [19] have reported that unlike cyanine dye HITCP, here NB accepts an electron from CND and forms NB•
− anion radical and the prompt appearance of transient species can be confirmed from a prominent positive signal at ∼423 and ∼520 nm for both in bulk water and in P123 micelle and shown in Fig. 7c, d. Similarly, the positive signal is found at ∼422 and ∼519 nm when NB is added after the incorporation of CND in BmimOs micelle, shown in Fig. S7b. In aqueous solution the transient species formed at ∼520 nm shows maximum growth after 0.2 ps delay time whereas in P123 and BmimOs micellar aggregate it needs comparatively longer time of 0.4 and 0.7 ps, respectively followed by the decay of the transient species. Hence, similar to the HITCP, for NB the electron transfer also requires relatively more time in the confined media of P123 and BmimOs micelle compared to bulk water i.e., the surfactants effectively retard the electron transfer. Here the ground state bleaching (GSB) and the stimulated emission (SE) peaks appear at ∼630 nm and shown in Fig. S8b.The change in absorbance (ΔOD) vs delay time profiles monitored for HITCP and NB in water, P123 micelle and BmimOs micelle are shown in Fig. S9a-f. We have performed steady state fluorescence and TCSPC measurements by exciting the samples at 340 nm and therefore the transient absorption spectra of CND at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm in water in the presence of HITCP and Nile blue are also executed and shown in Fig. S10a,b. It is found that the peak maxima of the transient species and time scale of its appearance are similar to that of the obtained from the 632 nm excitation.

Here, it is important to mention that we have excited, CND and 15 μM dye molecules (HITCP and NB) individually at 632 nm and no positive signal is found in the above mentioned wavelength regime and it clearly indicates that the appearance of the positive signal is due to the formation of a transient species after the PET between CND and dye molecules. We have also found that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of NB is higher than HITCP and the electron transfer dynamics is also faster for CND-NB pair compared to CND-HITCP pair in bulk water and inside P123 and BmimOs micelle. This observation infers the stronger electron acceptance efficiency of NB from CND compared to the electron donor property of HITCP dye to CND. Therefore, it is clear that the PET dynamics is slowed down in P123 and BmimOs micelle compared to bulk water due to the intervention of the surfactant molecules and this slow down is more for anionic BmimOs micelle compared to neutral P123 micelle due to the presence of electrostatic force of attraction between the surface of the anionic BmimOs micelle and cationic dye molecules. Pal and co-workers [48] have reported similar slowdown in the PET dynamics between dimethylaniline and cationic oxazine 1 fluorophore in anionic sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) and neutral Triton X-100 (TX-100) micelles compared to bulk water. Moreover, they have shown that the retardation in PET dynamics is more for SDS micelle compared to TX-100 micelle.

To obtain the further confirmation about the PET dynamics, ΔOD vs delay time profiles at the wavelength of appearance of maximum wavelengths are fitted using bi-exponential fitting equation and tabulated in Table S2. The fast time scale i.e., τ 1, represents the PET dynamics and for both the dye (HITCP and NB) addition, this PET dynamics is fastest in bulk water and slowest in BmimOs micelle. However, the τ 1value is relatively less for NB addition compared to HITCP addition and indicates the faster PET dynamics in the presence of NB dye compared to HITCP. The CNDs can be distributed in the micellar core as well as in the surface of the micelle. However, as the CNDs contain hydrophilic functional groups like carbonyl >(CO), hydroxyl (OH), etc.; therefore most of the CND molecules reside in the micellar surface. Moreover, the electron transfer between the CND and the fluorescent dye molecules (HITCP and Nile Blue) take place in a very fast time scale (∼1.5–10 ps) (Table S2) which indicates that the donor-acceptor pair are present in very close proximity and as the cationic dyes are present in the micellar surface, the CNDs are also present mainly in the micellar surface. Here the PET is occurring on the micellar surface where the donor and acceptor molecules can freely diffuse laterally over the surface of the micelle. Fayer and coworkers have shown the importance of diffusion in PET dynamics on a micellar surface using Monte Carlo simulation study [60]. Diffusion of the micellar aggregates themselves are insignificant on the time scale of PET dynamics compared to the lateral diffusion of the donor acceptor pair over the micellar surface [61]. PET dynamics gets slowed down inside P123 and BmimOs micelle compared to bulk water due to the intervention of the surfactant molecules between CND and the dye molecules. Both the donor and acceptor pair gets confined inside the micellar chain and subsequently the electron coupling matrix element reduces.

Literature reports suggest that the electron-transfer quenching of various fluorophores can take place from excited singlet state as well as from excited triplet state [62–65]. In the photochemistry of organic molecules, excited triplet states are important intermediates. However, the deactivation of the triplet state via different photophysical processes are generally very slow (typically in the order of microsecond) compared to the singlet state (in the order of nanosecond time scale) as triplet to singlet transitions are spin forbidden. In the present study, the electron transfer quenching takes place in picosecond (ps) to nanosecond (ns) time scale (shown in Table S2) and therefore electron-transfer quenching most probably taking place from excited singlet state and not from the triplet state. In this study, in the presence of Nile blue (NB), CND behaves as an electron donor whereas it (CND) acts as an electron acceptor in the presence of HITCP and the electron transfer pathways are shown in scheme 2 .

We have investigated the interaction between the carbon nanodot (CND) and cationic dye molecules (cyanine dye HITCP and phenoxazine dye Nile blue) in bulk water and in the P123 micelle. Interestingly, we have found that the addition of both the dye molecules (up to 15 μM) quench the fluorescence intensity of the CND. There is no decrease in the lifetime of CND in the presence of the dyes (HITCP or Nile blue) and thus the quenching process does not pass through FRET mechanism. Moreover, no change in the fluorescence lifetime of CNDs after the dye molecule addition proposes the static quenching pathway. Transient absorption measurement reveals that a new excited state transient radical species (cation or anion) generates through the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between the CND and the fluorophores and this PET pathway is responsible for fluorescence quenching. However, it is found that quenching of fluorescence intensity is more dominant in P123 micelle compared to bulk water as the surfactant molecules intervene and retard the PET process. Moreover, it can be concluded that the electron acceptance capability of NB is higher compared to the electron donation property of HITCP dye to CND.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: N.S. is thankful to SERB, Department of Science and Technology (DST), Government of India, for generous research grants. R.D. and A.P. are thankful to CSIR for the research fellowship and S.B. is thankful to IIT Kharagpur for the research fellowship. Authors would like to acknowledge SGDRI-UPM project of IIT Kharagpur for necessary equipment support. We are thankful to Dr. Chiranjib Banerjee at the initial phase of the experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.03.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
